questions.question: What is the fundamental difference between Flexbox and Grid?
options: Flexbox is one-dimensional; Grid is two-dimensional
Flexbox is for mobile; Grid is for desktop
questions.explanation: Flexbox operates along one axis (row or column), while Grid works with rows and columns simultaneously.

questions.question: Which tool is content-first (items drive sizing)?
questions.explanation: Flexbox is content-first — items decide their size based on content. Grid is layout-first — tracks define available space.

questions.question: Which tool is best for a page-level layout with header, sidebar, main, and footer?
questions.explanation: Page layouts are two-dimensional by nature (rows AND columns), making Grid the better choice for defining the overall page structure.

questions.question: When should you use Flexbox instead of Grid?
options: When you need to distribute items along a single axis
When you need both rows and columns
questions.explanation: Flexbox excels at single-axis distribution. If you only need a row or a column, Flexbox is simpler than Grid.

questions.question: True or False: You should choose either Grid or Flexbox and use it for your entire layout.
options: False — most production layouts combine both
True — pick one and stick with it
Only if the layout is simple
questions.explanation: The best CSS developers use Grid for overall page structure and Flexbox inside grid cells for component layouts.

questions.question: What is the recommended pattern for combining Grid and Flexbox?
options: Grid for the page layout structure, Flexbox inside cells for components
Flexbox for the page layout, Grid for components
questions.explanation: The standard pattern: Grid defines the overall layout (two-dimensional), and Flexbox handles component internals (one-dimensional).

questions.question: Which tool is better for creating a navigation bar?
options: Either — they are identical for nav bars
questions.explanation: Navigation bars are one-dimensional (horizontal or vertical). Flexbox handles content-driven widths and alignment naturally.

questions.question: Which tool is better for a card grid where cards must align perfectly in both directions?
options: Grid — items align to rows AND columns
Flexbox with flex-wrap — creates natural wrapping
questions.explanation: Grid creates a true two-dimensional grid where items align in both directions. Flexbox with flex-wrap leaves uneven last rows.

questions.question: What problem does Flexbox have for card grids that Grid solves?
options: Last row items stretch to fill the row, creating uneven sizes
questions.explanation: When using flex-wrap, items on the last row stretch to fill available space, creating awkward visual unevenness. Grid keeps all items properly aligned.

questions.question: Which tool is more appropriate for a tag list with different length labels?
questions.explanation: Tag lists need items to size to their content and wrap naturally — Flexbox handles this perfectly. Grid would over-constrain the items.

questions.question: Which is the simpler tool for centering content both horizontally and vertically?
questions.explanation: While Grid has `place-items: center` (shorter syntax), Flexbox is the more conventional and widely used tool for centering single items.

questions.question: When would you choose Grid over Flexbox for a two-column layout?
options: When you need precise column alignment and spanning
When you have only two items
Never — Flexbox is always better for two columns
questions.explanation: Both work for two-column layouts, but Grid gives you more control over column spanning and alignment.

questions.question: What is the main strength of Flexbox compared to Grid?
questions.explanation: Flexbox's main strength is one-dimensional layouts where items drive sizing based on their content — navigation, button groups, and wrapping lists.

questions.question: What is the main strength of Grid compared to Flexbox?
options: True two-dimensional layout with explicit row and column control
It is newer so it must be better
It replaces all uses of Flexbox
questions.explanation: Grid's main strength is two-dimensional layout where you control both rows and columns explicitly.

questions.question: Which tool should you use to create overlapping elements?
options: CSS Grid — items can occupy the same grid cells
questions.explanation: Grid's explicit cell model allows multiple items to occupy the same grid area, making overlap trivial. Flexbox cannot do this naturally.

questions.question: For a form where labels and inputs must align across multiple rows, which tool is better?
options: CSS Grid — grid-template-columns aligns label + input columns
Flexbox — with fixed widths on labels
Form elements cannot use CSS Grid
questions.explanation: Forms need two-dimensional alignment — labels in one column, inputs in another, aligned across multiple rows. Grid handles this perfectly.

questions.question: What happens with `flex: 1 1 280px` on items in a flex-wrap container?
options: Items are at least 280px, can grow, and wrap to new rows when the container is too narrow
Items are exactly 280px and overflow if needed
questions.explanation: `flex: 1 1 280px` = grow (1), shrink (1), basis (280px). Items start at 280px, grow to fill space, shrink if needed, and wrap when the container is too narrow.

questions.question: Which layout pattern is NOT possible with Flexbox alone?
options: Elements spanning multiple rows and columns simultaneously
A navigation bar with a logo and links
questions.explanation: Items spanning both rows and columns simultaneously (e.g., featured article spanning 2 cols × 2 rows) is a two-dimensional pattern that requires Grid.

questions.question: What is a good rule of thumb for choosing Grid vs Flexbox?
options: Use whichever requires fewer lines of CSS
questions.explanation: The simplest heuristic: if you need only a row or column, use Flexbox. If you need both rows and columns, use Grid.

questions.question: True or False: Grid and Flexbox cannot be nested inside each other.
options: False — they nest perfectly. Grid can contain Flexbox items and vice versa.
questions.explanation: Grid and Flexbox nest seamlessly. A Grid item can be a Flexbox container, and a Flexbox item can be a Grid container. This is the standard pattern for production layouts.
